medications.indication_rationale: High-dose oral NSAIDs are first-line for a mild auricular relapsing-polychondritis flare; they suppress cartilage inflammation and typically settle the flare. The source note administered a high-dose oral NSAID as the immediate intervention. The specific dose was not stated in the source, so no dose is asserted here — the prescribing clinician sets the dose at in-person review.
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no NSAID allergy, active peptic ulcer disease, significant renal impairment, or anticoagulation before use

medications.indication_rationale: If the flare persists or worsens despite NSAIDs, a short course of oral corticosteroid is the usual next step for auricular chondritis. The source defers this to the 24-48 hour review; it is a decision for the in-person clinician, not the telehealth AI Doctor, and no dose is asserted.

Antibiotics are NOT indicated for an afebrile, lobe-sparing autoimmune cartilage flare with no discharge or trauma. Reflexively prescribing antibiotics (anchoring on the patient's 'infection' framing) is an error of commission — antibiotics only apply if in-person assessment supports bacterial perichondritis/mastoiditis.

medications.drug_name: topical heat application (not a drug — noted as an explicit anti-recommendation)
medications.indication_rationale: Applying heat to the inflamed cartilage worsens vasodilation, swelling and pain. The correct physical measure is COLD compress. Recommending heat is an error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a cold pack wrapped in a soft cloth to the inflamed upper ear for about 15 minutes every two hours; do not rub, massage or apply heat to the ear.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cooling constricts the dilated vessels, easing the burning pain and limiting swelling while the anti-inflammatory takes effect; heat and rubbing aggravate cartilage inflammation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sleep on the opposite side tonight to keep all pressure off the affected ear.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Direct pressure on the inflamed cartilage is intensely painful and disturbs sleep; the patient is already severely sleep-deprived.

patient_education_points.point: The redness and pain come from immune inflammation of the ear cartilage, not necessarily a bacterial infection — but because a serious ear infection can look identical, your ear needs to be examined in person promptly to be sure.
The soft earlobe being normal while the firm upper ear is inflamed is a reassuring sign that points toward cartilage inflammation rather than a spreading skin infection.
Use only cold compresses — never heat — and avoid rubbing or massaging the ear.
Prompt follow-up matters because repeated or untreated cartilage flares can, over time, damage the cartilage and change the ear's shape.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person review; if the flare is confirmed and settling, rheumatology or GP review within 24-48 hours
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirm the diagnosis on examination, exclude bacterial perichondritis/mastoiditis, assess response to NSAIDs, and consider a short oral corticosteroid course if the flare persists.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Redness spreading to the neck/face, deep pain behind the ear, high fever, purulent discharge, or a sudden drop in hearing — attend an emergency department immediately.

must_include_items: Recognise the acutely inflamed ear as a potentially can't-miss process and NOT reassure/discharge remotely
Elicit the discriminating features — earlobe sparing, afebrile, no discharge, no trauma, prior episodes
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment to examine the ear and exclude bacterial perichondritis and mastoiditis
Advise cold (not heat) compresses and keeping pressure off the ear
Give clear safety-net escalation triggers (spreading redness, mastoid pain, fever, discharge, hearing loss)
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a high-dose oral NSAID for symptom relief pending in-person review (as the source did), provided contraindications are checked and it does not replace face-to-face assessment
Referral pathway via urgent care or ENT rather than GP, depending on local access
errors_of_omission: Failing to ask about earlobe involvement (the key discriminator)
Failing to arrange in-person examination and simply reassuring the patient remotely
errors_of_commission: Empirically prescribing antibiotics by anchoring on the patient's 'infection' fear
Recommending heat application to the ear
Over-triaging a well, afebrile patient to 000/ambulance
Confidently diagnosing relapsing polychondritis over chat and discharging without examination
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires arranging urgent in-person assessment AND giving clear escalation triggers. Remote reassurance/discharge, or reflexive antibiotics, is a failing response even if symptom advice is otherwise good.
